text:
Most companies calling themselves "API-first" mean they have APIs.

The gap shows up the moment you ask a different question: who is the API designed for?

For the last decade, the answer was a human developer reading docs, copying a curl command, and stitching things together. Reasonable. That was the buyer.

The buyer is changing. A meaningful share of integrations next year will start with an AI agent, not a person. Agents don't read your getting-started guide. They probe, reason about schemas, and give up when discovery is ambiguous.

Most APIs fail that test. Endpoints are named for internal data models. Auth flows assume a browser. Error messages help humans debug, not machines retry. Pagination is inconsistent across the same product.

The teams I've watched pull ahead aren't shipping more endpoints. They're redesigning the consumption layer: predictable schemas, machine-readable capability descriptions, semantic error codes, idempotency by default.

If an agent had to complete a transaction through your product tonight, with no human in the loop, would it succeed on the first try?

That's the bar now.

#APIFirst #DeveloperExperience #SaaS #ProductStrategy #B2BSaaS #TechProduct

diagram.title: THE AGENT-READY API STACK
diagram.subtitle: Five layers that separate API-as-checkbox from API-as-moat

steps.label: DISCOVERABILITY
steps.description: Agents find capabilities without reading human documentation.

steps.label: SEMANTIC SCHEMAS
steps.description: Field names describe meaning, not internal database structure.

steps.label: DETERMINISTIC ERRORS
steps.description: Machine-readable codes that tell an agent what to retry.

steps.label: IDEMPOTENT ACTIONS
steps.description: Repeated calls produce the same outcome, every time.

steps.label: AUTONOMOUS TRANSACTION
steps.description: Agent completes the job with no human in the loop.
